Okay, deep breaths. All right, good morning everybody. We have a ton of questions to go through and what we're gonna try to do is just, I think, I guess I'll read the questions quickly and then, Andre, you can answer and I'll answer and we'll just kind of see how it goes from there, if that makes sense. Anything to add before we get started? I feel like we've just got a workday is worth the work to do here that we need to dive into. Yeah, I think this makes sense. I think based on what I've seen of the questions, quite a few of the questions are asking for black and white answers and we may be disappointing some of you guys where they are no black and white answers. So yeah. That's the truth. All right, if I mispronounce your name when I read your questions, I'm just going to share a lot of first names. If I mispronounce your name, I apologize. It's not intentional. First person, the first question is coming from Guillermo. They're like 17 questions in this first question. So I'll just read it. I'm going to read all of them quickly and then Andre will just come back. I think we'll just go through them one at a time. So actually, rather than do that, I'll read them individually and then we'll answer the question. All right, first question. You quote Dean Jackson where he talks about for the last two years, that only 15% of people buy within the first 90 days, 85% of them buy after 90 days. How true do these stats hold for you, Andre and for Sean? How do these stats hold for when you were doing agency work, if you remember? Do you want to tackle this? You want me to tackle this? And there's a short answer to this one, I think. Yeah, go for it. Okay, so here's the short answer. Dean Jackson's not talking about Dean Jackson's clients here. He didn't go look at his customers. Dean didn't go look at his business and say, oh, that's interesting, 15% of my customer, that's Dean went and looked at all of his clients. Dean works with lots of clients with enormous lists. So this observation that a minority of people who buy over two years by within the first 90 days is broadly applicable. I've seen it happen, I don't know, with clients over and over and over again, I wouldn't specifically do that analysis, but that was in general, the signal that you get in the first 90 days is a very weak signal relative to the signal over two years. We had somebody message us the other day during the art of email promotion saying, I've been on your list for five years and seven months, this is my first purchase. So we've had others, and I think somebody published in one of the comments of breakdown of sort of exactly this phenomenon. I think that was someone who's a undamed financial publisher said, here's our breakdown. I mean, it's not, don't get hung up on 15% versus 85%. Focus on the fact that the very small minority of people who are going to buy from you eventually will buy in the first 90 days. Overwhelmingly, people who buy from you are going to buy after 90 days. That's the message, don't get hung up on the details. Anything to add? Is that Chris? You shared that, all right, sorry. Anything to add on that one? Andre, I just want to jump right to the next one. Yeah, I think that you've nailed it. It's directionally, it's important. And I think broadly speaking, lots of people never see that 85% piece because they're just hammering the first 15% and then people leave. So the rest never reveals itself. Well, the reality is, I mean, it's batshit crazy when you think about it, right? Where I was like, oh, we've got to do all these things to make the system squeeze the last drop out of this signal we're trying to get. It's stack on the bonus. It lets you do all these things to try to squeeze every last drop out of a tiny percentage of people who are actually going to buy. It makes no sense. The moment you recognize that overwhelmingly the opportunity is after 90 days, the moment you understand that, everything changes. That's the insight that we're trying to share here. All right, you can tell I'm gonna get amped up today. It's just over or so, whatever. I'll just apologize in advance. All right, let's move on to the second question. Yeah. You talk about the fact that the art of email is about how the parts work together and it is forever changing. What would you say is the connection between the art of email and retargeting? This is a traffic question, damn it. Like, do you use the world building framework when you build out retargeting strategies and will you guys be adding a module to talk about best practices for that? Do you wanna jump on this one at all before I rant? Yeah, retargeting is in my wheelhouse. Well, in retargeting, the art of email is about email. It's not about paid traffic. So we're not gonna talk about retargeting in the art of email. That's the short answer. But what your Guillermo is hinting at and making a connection to is what we're sort of internally referring to as the Holy Grail project, which it's not really even a project yet. It's just this thing, it's like a sliver in my brain that I can't let go, which is, you know, at some point we're probably going to put all of these things together into something that has the traffic component in it with the world building and the email because there's signals among all of these things. But that's not the art of email. And it's absolutely not what anyone should be focused on right now. Like, that's like fifth order thinking. Like once the email, you know, once you have built out a relationship building series, a value newsletter, a few soap opera series, you get your bridge emails, you get your PLS, like you get everything built, you know, phase one, year one, we'll come back and we can add retargeting at that point. But the relative value of doing that early, it's just not there. So I wouldn't have this on your radar. Yes, we're thinking about it. Yes, it's important. Yes, it will come. No, you should not be focusing on it right now. All right, question three. You talk about finding leverage points to create ways to maximize the use of all the parts of the art of email. Question is, do you have a practice of how when first working on a new product or with a new client that you use to define leverage points? I can't wait to hear your answer to this question. Yeah, mine's gonna be an in of one response because I've never had clients. So I can only reflect on how I've done this stuff in the past and more recently. And I think for somebody that's in their business, it becomes clearer where those leverage points are. I mean, if, and again, this is such an open-ended question. If you've been building a version of art of email for years, well, that's gonna be different to somebody that has a business that's fully, it's a full operating thing, there's multiple products and they're doing a different type of email marketing. Well, that's gonna be a very different type of question. I mean, answer because now it's, well, how do you wanna eat the elephant? You've got to identify maybe if there's a lot of promotions going on that a PLS would be a good place to start. Maybe one of the entry points, you could stick a relationship building one on there and start to bring people into this world in a way that's in line with what we teach inside of you. So I don't know, yeah, I don't know how Sean's dealt with this with clients in the past, but it's not an easy answer. What's not an obvious answer if you're not in the business? Yeah, and my challenge is I am hyper, hyper rational and I've had people call me that and I realized after the fact that many of them were not saying it as a compliment. So my brain, I don't know how to not think this way and I try to. And so for me, the first thing I'm trying to figure out when I'm looking for leverage points is what am I optimizing for? And this is with clients, this is with us, this is for me personally. And it's shocking to me having worked with nearly 250 clients, it is shocking to me how few people can answer that question accurately and precisely. I've said this, I think I said this in traffic engine, I've said this before on Q and A calls, but if you can't draw your business on a single sheet of paper with a crayon, you don't understand your business. And I don't say that glibly, like I mean it, like if you can't sit down and say, here's the audience, here's the offer, here's the mechanism by which the audience is exposed to the offer, but if you can't do that, you don't understand it. So for me, this finding leverage begins with this question, what am I optimizing for? What am I trying to accomplish? And then where this is where I get dysfunctional because it's paralyzing to me when the actions being taken have no intellectual causal relationship with the outcomes that are desired. And that working with clients for a very long time was stunning to me how often the proposed solution had no relationship to what was trying to be achieved other than someone thought maybe that they were related somehow, right? And it baffles me. And there are frameworks for figuring this out, the theory of constraints is a really good, I mean, it's not a simple framework, you need to spend some time really understanding it, but at a very basic level, theory of constraints is probably the best framework I've ever found for this. And in the absence of that, I would say first ask yourself, what is it that I'm trying to accomplish? Like how do I know when I'm successful? And you need to define that with some precision, right? And but at the same time, you need to define that in a way that's meaningful because too much precision, you can get down to the level of, oh, my definition of success is if I get a hundred leads per day, bullshit. You don't want leads, nobody's in the leads business unless you're a lead aggregator, you resell leads, you're in the, you sell something, right? You're in the customer business. So it's, you don't want a hundred leads, you want X number of customers and it's not X number of customers, it's X number of happy customers. Like you have to really understand what the hell is it I'm trying to do here? And oftentimes that becomes like, often it's a Venn diagram where there are a set of characteristics that have to be true. For example, you might need to generate a baseline income. And in addition to that, there might be certain ways that you generate, like things you will or won't do to generate that income. And then there's a certain experience of the work that you want to have and all of those things overlap in this bullseye center that's like, oh, that's my definition of success. Well, then that kind of tells you the space to go look for leverage points. It's not a very elegant answer, but the way my brain works is very much, I just imagine myself going through this process of, it's rational, it's logical, it's will this achieve the thing? I want X to be true, I'm going to do Y, does Y create X? Like that's just, it sounds so, so batched crazy, but that's just how my brain works. And for me, the profound dysfunction is that if I can't connect those two dots, I'm paralyzed, which is not great for working with clients incidentally. So that's a very long answer. I don't know if it's a great answer, but there is no easy answer to this question, but it's incredibly important. You have to be able to look at the business as a system and say, this is what this system is supposed to produce. Like with this, if all the things are working well, it's going to do X, so it's going to produce 10 happy customers every day or whatever it is. And then go back and look and see, like what is the thing here that's most likely to be slowing that down? Or what is an initiative that I can take? And I think the last thing to look forward to is to make sure that you're surfacing all truths. This is one thing that I've observed is that people will say like, this is the priority. And then they'll make decisions with a different criteria because the real priority has not been externalized. So for example, I saw this happen a lot with clients where say, the priority is X number of customers, but then the budget would be distributed among different divisions of a company, sort of politically, like everybody needed a slice of the pie. So when it came time to spend money, for example, on advertising, the idea that we were optimizing for customers was subordinate to the idea that everybody got a piece of the pie, but nobody talked about the fact that everybody got a piece of the pie. So by not actually externalizing that, it was impossible to find the leverage points because there were these unspoken things going. So the first thing you have to do is be honest. And be honest about things like, what does this business need to produce for me to feel comfortable with it from an economic standpoint? Like, okay, start there and be honest. Don't say it needs to make a million dollars. No, what does it have to do to be so that you're able to focus on the business comfortably without being distracted by money? Okay, start there. And then what are the things you are or are not willing to do? Like where are your red lines? Where are the things that you won't do? And then what do you want your experience of the work to be? You know, really focus on kind of the big things like that you don't want to do. Like you don't want to work 12 hours a day or maybe you do it first or whatever. Get all of that clear and you'll start to find that in that solution space, the leverage points become a lot clearer, a lot faster. Okay, sorry. Next question, this is from Rich. Well, let's see. So here's the quote from the email, not the course. And then he has some questions. This is the quote, what we think of as our business isn't a separate thing independent of how we create awareness, engagement, conversion. Instead, our businesses emerge from the way that those parts of our systems interact. Now this question, is that mold this over and I might be overthinking it, but from which side of the experience do you mean? Does a business emerge from them? Me and the customer or prospect or us or both? And then there are other questions. So I'll just read the remaining questions that seem to relate to those questions. Do you mean this literally for us as in the business product is created from customer interactions with those three spheres? Like you build the relationships deep in the relationships based on their actions and then build the offer? No. Or do you mean that, sorry, I can't help myself with answering as we go. Or do you mean that this business as revealed for them like a magic box that you only arrive at by going through these interactions, build a relationship, any and no? All right, Andre, you answer this and then I'll try to limit my answer after. So the question is, does the business emerge for them or us or both when we talk about the business as an emergent property of awareness, engagement, conversion? Yeah, I'll answer it in a way that's, it's kind of linked to your previous answer, which is every business has these three layers which we talk about in one of the modules. So goal, vision, strategy and in tactics. And so we start at the top, we start with the goal for the business and our vision. And as Sean said just now, the red lines that we're not willing to do, this is how we choose to show up and do things. And then you come up with a strategy on how you're gonna execute on a goal. And so if I look at this through our lens in how we building TLB, we have clarity on that top piece and we kind of know what we want, how we wanna show up in the world and do our strategy part. Now the tactics are the pieces that can change day to day, week to week, month to month. But there's so much stuff that we don't know, we don't know what we don't know. So when we executing on our goals, on our goal vision and we executing on that strategy, things happen that were not obvious to us. So when we do certain things and when certain parts of how we do email marketing, how we do this stuff on the website, how those two things interact together, they create immersion results that neither one of us would have been able to come up with had we not, you know, slam these pieces together and for them to interact in a way that's, it just did and this result came out of that. So I think that's my broad response to that. And I'll approach I think from a slightly different way. So what we meant in that quote is, there is no business separate from its parts and there is no part within those parts that's the business, right? And that, again, I'd realize that sounds crazy. Well, let's think about this. So we advertise on Facebook, is that our business? Well, no, our business isn't advertising on Facebook but we do that to generate awareness, okay? Well, we have, we do lead generation as part of that. We have people who come into our world who read our emails. Is that our business? Well, we're not in the email business. That's what that's part of our business. And now there's a relationship between the people who see an ad on Facebook and what they see in the email. So from those two things emerges an experience for somebody in a result for us that is not contained in either of those things. And that's really a print. That's a fundamental idea of systems theory is that the individual parts don't contain the business. The interactions of those parts are where the business emerges. And when you put awareness, engagement and conversion, generally speaking, those are three irreducible parts of a business. You cannot have a business. If you remove awareness, you have a business that nobody knows about. So there's nobody, there's no there there. If you have awareness, but you don't have engagement, then you have, again, you don't have a business. There's people, there's a lot to do. You can see tons of stuff on whatever Instagram, but there's no way to then take the next step into that person's world. That's not a business. And if you only have conversion, if you have something to sell, you have all the systems to sell it, but you have no engagement, no awareness, you have no, there's no business. And so the point is when you have all three of these things, the thing that we call a business emerges from the interaction of all three and does not exist anywhere independently in those three things. And the next evolution of that thinking is how you do each of those parts has a dramatic effect on the whole. So if we decided that we wanted to do high volume lead gen, promising, let's just say for the sake of argument, we wanted to do really high volume lead generation with a, to lower our cost per lead as much as possible, no emphasis on quality. And we were promising a million freebies, right? So we have, you get 10 special reports in the video series and all of this in exchange for your email. Do we think that would have an effect on the rest of our business considering how we teach and what we teach? Absolutely, right? Because it would be, it would not be congruent with everything else that we do. So it would change the entire system. We would probably get, I'm guessing, a lot more leads of a lot lower quality, which would change everything about our business. And it would not be congruent because we don't teach giving bonuses to capture attention. So the point here is there is nothing that we can point to and say, this is the business. And we work on this independently of the way we generate awareness, engagement and conversion. What we think of as a business is an emergent property that does not exist independent of all of those things. I mean, Andre and I could decide today that we want to go open a business quote unquote in whatever, you know, pick your topic and we could create a bunch of content. We could do all kinds of things with just the two of us interacting and producing things and having the best course in the world about whatever it is ready to sell. But in the absence of all of the other parts, there's no business yet. It's an expensive hobby at that point until we have awareness, until we have engagement. And then from all of those component parts, what we think of as a business would emerge. That's a very long answer. All right, my head hurts now. Let's move on to Guillermo's next question. This is from about the forward. Sean Platt talks about not considering himself as an internet marketer. I can completely relate to this. This is the question. Do you see as things are changing the depth of the typical agency consultant that only focuses on the lowest paid click and opt-ins? In that it is crucial to make a shift to being more strategic and creating a mini presence that is anchored by a highly cared for and engaged email list. I love this question. You're asking the two people who teach this very concept if we think this concept is good. So I can't wait to hear you answer this, Andre. How do you feel about the way we teach internet marketing, Andre? Yes. Yeah, it's, I mean, the world's always gonna exist with extremes on both sides and a spectrum somewhere in the middle that ranges. And I don't think that's gonna change. There's always gonna be hard call, copyright or direct response to doing their stuff until they honestly can't do it anymore. And there's always gonna be people on the other side that do completely other things, different things. And yeah, I don't know. I mean, you as a consultant agency person would need to decide how you're gonna show up. I mean, so be it for me to give advice on agency stuff because I've never done it before. But I think if I ever had an agency or I was a consultant, I would be a certain type of one. And this is how I show up. And if the stuff that I'm consulting about or showing you how to build doesn't resonate with you, that's not my job, my job necessarily to convince you that this is the way forward. And I wouldn't be taking on clients and just doing stuff that clients want me to do that is incongruent with how I wanna show up. I think I'm rambling now, but yeah. Yeah, I don't have a good answer then. Yeah, I mean, I think that the short answer to this question is no. So do you see as things are changing the death of a typical agency consultant that only focuses on the lowest pay clicks and opt-ins? No, there's always gonna be room for that business model. And it works. The reason we don't teach that business model is because of what's required to make that business model work. I mean, when you go into the boardrooms of some of these really big direct response marketers and you see what's required, I mean, their business models in general are based on two principles, it's probably the wrong idea, but two ideas. One, they want to recoup somewhere in the neighborhood of 80% of the cost to acquire a customer in the first 30 days. Like that's a benchmark. And then they want to make their profit, measuring LTV, lifetime value, somewhere in the neighborhood of the next six months, some of them define it all the way up to a year, some even longer. But when you have a business that's structured that way, so you need to get, again, two points, you need to make back approximately 80% of the cost to acquire a customer in the first 30 days, and you then make all of your money in the next six months. That structure necessitates a certain type of business. And it necessitates a certain approach to things like traffic. And if you have the resources to make that business model work, like you have the ability to go out and buy traffic and not need to recoup the cost of that traffic over 30 days, and you only need 80% of the cost of that traffic back to feel comfortable about making money, but that business model works. We never suggest it doesn't work. It's just a really difficult business model to use if you're a creative professional just getting started. And there's an assumption that's sort of out there in the broader internet marketing world, that that's the only way to do it, right? Because the internet marketing sort of has, it's the early days of internet marketing were very much influenced by direct response marketing. And direct response marketing has some sort of intellectual overhead. It creates a certain set of needs that in the, from our perspective, we just don't think it's the best fit, particularly for creative professionals, early entrepreneurs, people just getting started. Now, if you're sitting on $100 million and or $10 million or $5 million, and you want to go out and invest in buying traffic and making a return on investment on that traffic, there are ways to do that. And there are consultants who focus on that. And we're not suggesting that there's anything inherently wrong with that. There is no capital T truth in the world from our perspective. There are, what we say occasionally is that we believe in the value of subjective utility versus objective reality. I mean, we don't care if it's capital T truth, we care if it's capital U useful. So what we try to do is look at our audience and say knowing what we know, based on our experience, four plus decades doing this, what do we think is the best approach for the people who we serve to get the results we know that we want? And a direct response model, traditional direct response model is just it's suboptimal to get those results. So there's always going to be room. We sit at a very big table. And there are people who have entirely different approaches than we do. And they're not wrong and we're not right. Everybody's right, but everyone's right in a different context. So the second part of the question is that crucial to make a shift to being more strategic and creating a mini presence that is anchored by a highly cared for or engaged email list. We believe for our audience, that is a better approach. It has lower financial overhead. It resonates more with who we are as individuals. It seems to resonate more with who our audience tends to be in their projects. We think that makes sense. We think certainly if you want to be a consultant, there's room for that as well. But be aware that's a lot harder to be that kind of consultant and a lot harder to be that kind of agency than it is to go in and promise clients high volume numbers that they can understand, lower cost per lead, higher number of leads. That's a whole lot easier to sell. Selling quality is really, really hard. And selling quality over time is infinitely harder. So again, no right answer, no wrong answer. There's our answer. There's our answer in a certain context. But we believe our answer for our audience is the best fit. It's the most likely to be optimal. All right, module one, foundations, introduction, Guillermo. You talk about how the art of email will show us how to segment people and understand how to find those who are ready to buy right now. Can the art of email be used for a business that specifically needs right now business and doesn't have the luxury of creating a long runway for building relationships? You first, Andre. I'm just gonna throw this out and say that implementing the art of email is not a fit for every business. If a business wants to operate in a certain way or it's a certain type of business, then the art of email may not be a good fit. So the first thing that come to mind is E-com. When somebody is browsing for a widget, for a pair of glasses, whatever, right? And there's many versions of that thing. Perhaps the relationship in that context is, with air quotes, less important. Someone wants to find the thing that they're looking for and then buy it at a reasonable price. If it's something that's a commodity, if it's something special like what Apple sell, well then they're the only place to get it. So yeah. I think, yeah, that's my initial response. Yeah, and I think, I mean, there's another thing to think, I mean, I get the question. It's a really important question. But here's one way I would think about answering that. I'm not sure all the businesses we think of are right now businesses are actually right now businesses. I don't know, I didn't see the list that Dean Jackson used to figure out the 15 versus 85% distribution. But my guess is that a lot of businesses on that list have offers that they would consider right now offers and right now businesses. And lots of people do their business that way. So the way I would think about this is most likely a very small fraction of the audience you're likely to get to buy is a right now buyer. And I'll use myself as an example just to, I don't know if this will be a perfect example, but years, several years, a couple of years ago, two, three years ago, I listened to a podcast and they referenced red light therapy as recovery for strength training athletes. Mentioned a particular company, Juve, J-O-O-V-V. They use this with their athletes, profound results. So I immediately went and looked at Juve site. Like after I listened, I think I was seeing in the car, I looked at it. I think it was 18 months or two years later when I finally purchased from them. You know, they're an E-com company. So right now purchase, right? You go and you look and you're like, oh, I like these. But I purchased a lot much later. And that's been true. It's generally true for how I purchased a lot of things. So I purchased a Traeger grill last year, very similar. Heard about Traeger, started to see it all over the place. Bumped into one, went to their site, found out there's a retailer nearby. Like all of these weird things happen. Grill is a grill of right now business, I think so. But most people that, I think the insight here is that most people who buy don't buy right away. That's just a fundamental truth of business. Now, sometimes we do with certain things, sure that the cycle is compressed. But I think in general, this distribution of minority of purchases early, majority of purchases later, is the model for us, almost every business that you're going to run into. So if that's true, and we embrace that as an idea, then what we need, then it just becomes a matter of what are the consequences of that being true? If we know that a lot of people are going to engage with us now, and then not buy for a long time, no matter what we do, then it changes how we interact with our customers. That's just, to me, that just seems like the logical extension of that realization. This gets back to one of the earlier questions about looking for leverage points. And this is how my brain works, and I want to be clear about it, because I don't think this is necessarily the right way to think about things. But it's how I think about things and how I can't not think about things this way, which is once we establish that something is true, what then is, how should we act if that's true? That's how my brain functions. I can't not do this. So if it's true that the majority of people who buy from us, the significant majority of people who buy from us, no matter what we do, are not going to buy until after 90 days, within a couple of years. And Dean wasn't talking about Dean, like he and I are good friends. Dean Jackson was not talking about everybody, all the leads on your list, X number of them buy in the first 90 days. He's very specific about what he said. Of the people who buy, 15% purchase in the first 90 days and the remainder purchase, so it's customers. So we know from this observation, this broad observation, that in general, people who are going to buy from you are going to buy later. From that one insight, to me, to me it's one of the most profound insights I've heard, is from that insight, the downstream effects are profound. We start to change our interaction with people. If we know for every hundred people reading our first email, who later buy, 85% of them aren't going to buy for at least three months, that single insight changes everything about what we do. So that's a very long answer to that question. All right, let's move on to the relationship. I like that. It's worth saying that, Bar right now can also, that timeline can be, it can be sooner rather than later, meaning that somebody can still go through a sequence and then be presented with an opportunity to bar. And in that moment, or thereabouts, that person can decide that they're gonna become a customer, but that transaction is never done. It's not done in the moment, it's done later, but they've made that mental leap that, I like these guys or their stuff, how they're showing up, and I wanna be a customer. I'm just not ready right now. So, which is why we've unpacked that principle, right in module number one, is because it's important and it's impossible to know how many people decide they're gonna become a customer before any transaction. And it can be long before, I mean, like Sean's just mentioned now, I mean, it can be years later, but maybe he had an inkling that that do thing was something that was a good fit. Just in that moment, it wasn't a perfect fit. I mean, there's a company, I used to visit them ages ago, Jay Peterman Company, I think it is. It's worth going and getting a look at their stuff and they're not our type of business, but it's an e-com store that sell clothing and but every single product on there has a story attached to it. And I can just imagine that the people that resonate with that brand before they buy anything, they always read that story and that's associated with whatever and they internalize it and enjoy the moment of reading the story that's associated with something that they're about to buy. And there's some excitement in that and then they'll make the purchase. And so, okay, so that's me done. I think there's one other point to make it two actually. Somebody R.C. posted in the chat, study your own buying behaviors, absolutely. Watch yourself how you buy things and you'll realize how convoluted it is when people buy. I mean, this notion that we can, even with the most sophisticated tools, that we accurately capture the reality of buying is laughable. We see the little signals that can be measured in this vast sea of context and data. So that's one part. But the other thing to think about here too is embedded in this question is that there's an assumption. There's an assumption that somehow we can affect the reality by making our business different, right? That there's a type of business that's a right now business and that business, that their results are different. And I don't, I want to be cautious about that because what we're really talking about is the world works a certain way. And we're adapting what we do based on the reality of the way the world works. We're not trying to carve out a slice of the world and bend it to our will and say, well, if we can just change it this way and we can sort of, right? I mean, let's just assume for the sake of argument that we're in an audience, a market where 20% of the people who would buy over in the course of two years will buy the first 90 days and half of those people would buy it at the first contact. So we can have a Facebook ad that goes to a very intense funnel. We can remove all distraction and stack bonuses. And we can really drive a certain percentage of people through that. And maybe we could improve our results. Let's just say we could double our, we're so good that we could get, we could 2X our results relative to everybody else. The reality of the world is that we're 2Xing our results on a tiny minority of people who would buy anyway. That's the reality. We can't change that fundamental reality. Something, yes, there will be exceptions here and there, but just because we engineer the best sales funnel in the world, we read every book on persuasion and persuasion and coercion, and we put it all together and we're geniuses, we're still only going to get more of a very small number of people who will buy because overwhelmingly people are just not going to buy right now, it's human nature. And you're talking about leverage points, trying to affect human nature is not a high leverage point. We're not going to find it there. So our philosophy really is much more about, let's just see how the world really works and adapt what we do to get better results from that reality instead of wishing the reality were different. Okay, let's move on to the next question. This is from relationships before transactions, this is Guillermo. If you're just starting to build all this out, what order would you recommend in order to keep delivering on the relationship, keep people engaged? Let's see. Okay, I know from reading further on, you recommend to start with RBS and then send a relationship building series and then send them to a value newsletter and then build the soap opera sequences as branches that extend from the value newsletter. Is that the process you suggest? This isn't a question about relationships before transactions, actually. This is a question about later down the line. I'm going to skip this question, I'm going to move it because I think it's a question for later. That's been an excuse. Yeah, it's next week. And we're not doing that just to be clear to everybody, we're not doing that to be jerks, we're doing that because the idea here is that we're building, we are releasing the art of email in a very specific way to coincide with a very specific order of this workshop, because we know about all of the parts and we have a pretty good idea about how to best go through the parts so that you'll get maximum value from it. So when we say we're going to answer that later, it's not because we're lazy, it might be a little bit because we're lazy. It's mostly because we know if we answer that, we can very easily derail you now and get you thinking about things that you should be thinking about later without internalizing the things we want you to internalize now. So in knowing that we have a lot to get through, I think we'll just move on to the next one. All right, customers are created before they buy anything. One of our favorite topics, this is Guillermo. Here's the question, how do you know how much to give for free? When you're creating content for big, huge concepts, how do you know how much of that concept should you disclose before asking them to opt in or to pay, and is there a difference there as well? Like for content that's leading up to an opt in with no exchange of money, is that different than content that leads up to an exchange of money? Is the quality of the free content different or the threshold of how much you give different? Take it away on your end. It's worth recognizing the fact that nowhere inside of auto email do we talk about lead generation. So I just want to draw attention to that. And at the same time that the quality of the leads that come into a business, at which point, we've codified our thinking in auto email. When you do stuff before people opt in for the first time, that's one of the largest leverage points you can ever do. So getting low quality traffic, well, low quality leads onto an email list at which point auto email kind of plays out. If the quality of those leads is bad, there's nothing you can do in auto email that's going to change the effect. It'll be very difficult to push that rock up the hill as opposed to doing a lot of stuff on the front end before somebody opt in, which is really encoded inside of Sphere of Influence. So I'm not going to spend too much time talking about that other than the fact that we can affect how somebody becomes a prospect a lot before the opt in. So yeah, and then the question about free versus not free, you know, that seems like a binary thing, like, you know, what is too much free? And the way that we look at this is that this isn't free versus not free, or how much free versus we need to keep something back and hide it away behind a product or behind an email in an email. It's, we're building a world that people want to inhabit. So the people that we care about serving, that's the website that we built. And every single day we decide what we're going to create and we will share it and put it on that world or we'll put it into emails and send those emails out. At no point do we decide, well, you know, maybe we are sharing too much or too little, you know, we have courses that unpack our philosophies and our world-building efforts outside of that. We share as much as we possibly can without sharing entire courses, because, you know, you can't do that, but we share as much as we possibly can share without thinking about what's free and what's not free. That's my, that's my rambling. Yeah, I think, for me, you said, I think it's important to draw attention to, free versus not free is it's reductionist, it's linear, it's not systemic. And from a systems perspective, again, if we go back to this idea that our business is emerging from the interaction and the relationships of all the various parts, then it becomes very difficult to tease out an answer to this question, like, what's okay to share with the audience and how much, but what should we hold back? Like that gets very difficult because it's not just the individual thing that we're sharing, it's the thing as it relates to other things, which in the context in which they received it. So if you think about, like we, over the course of the summer, we wrote a lot of articles that we would send, we'd send an email every week, we switched to a weekly format this summer, so we would send emails every week, and many of those linked to a, your 2000 or so word article that was on our website. So that one piece of content has two very different contexts for people who are in our audience already, they experienced that content through email in a way that's probably passive, meaning they're reading their email, they get an email from us and it's like, oh, here's this thing, and then they go, they weren't looking for it, so they didn't have active intent. Other people find that same content, but they find it through organic search primarily, and they, but they were looking for something. So same content, two very different contexts. So how much should we give each of those people for free? Well, that's an interesting question because some of those people are, in our audience, in our email list, some of them are our customers already who are reading our emails. So, and when you start trying to buy for Kate, free versus paid, and this is really, I think it's interesting that this question was asked within the context of the customers are created before they buy anything, we don't, we really don't distinguish, and I can't emphasize this as enough. We're not paying lip service to this idea that treat everyone like a customer, and that's just, it just makes more sense. It's a perfectly rational thing to do because it's just too damn hard to try to figure out the 50 contexts somebody might consume content that we create. So when we create something, we're creating, I'm gonna borrow a term that Sam used in the chat because I love it, our friend Sue uses this term too, vignette, vignette is vine, it's like a thread. You can imagine like a vine, it's just a beautiful visual image for that. So our longer content, they're not really vignettes, but they're, it's just, it's content that, it's just lighter, right? And it's not because it's free necessarily, sometimes it's just, how much cognitive load does this require of our audience, right? We don't wanna be overly demanding to our audience. So 1500 words, 2000 words, kind of high level concept, like we wrote about the simple testing framework. It's just kind of, it's an interesting, someone can consume that and their life can be better as a result. Now, we're not planning on a deep dive course in analytics that that's set up, that's not it at all. It's just, this is kind of interesting to share with our audience. And we know a significant number of people who consume that content are paying customers. Some own one course, some own four courses, some own everything we produce the moment we create it. Others have been in our audience, they own nothing, but they have some affinity for us. They pay attention, which is an honor to have their attention. So we want something to be valuable for them too. Others have no idea who we are, and they stumble on that same thing, and it's their introduction to us. So it just seems like a rational decision to say, screw it, we're just gonna make everything really good. And yes, you can do, there's more you can get. And there's a point at which we ask it, for an exchange of monetary value to go even deeper. But we're not thinking about this, where's my checklist of quality? What am I willing to tell somebody? We treat people in our audience like their customers. Like there are, more importantly, we treat people in our audience like their friends. And when that idea, for example, for the simple testing framework appeared, it was like, okay, well, how do I share this idea with a friend? And when we write it, and we often, we have avatars who we have, who we think about when we write, those avatars are not composites, they're real people. They're real people in our audience who we have relationships with. So when we're creating some of this content in the back of the mind is, hey, I have this idea that's really interesting. I can't get out of my head. I really want to share it with, fill in the blank with this particular person. How would I explain that to him? Or how would I explain that to her? There's no thought about, well, but I better go check and see how much he's purchased in the last 90 days. It's just, who wants to do that? We don't want to do that. We've engineered our business not to do that. So important questions, I appreciate the questions being asked, but I would suggest not trying to think about it in those terms. If for no other reason, then it's just a lot easier to just create content that you're proud of and feel good. And the caveat here being, we recognize many of you are just getting started. And you're like, damn, that's a lot of work to create stuff for quote unquote free. Like how do I figure out how to do that? Just pick some percentage of your time to create and make that available. And that might be for you, you might tweet, right? That might be your thing and your sort of testing ideas. You might, there are people who post on Facebook, right? You might post long form content on Facebook, but that's whatever it is, that's world building, that's sharing, that's creating value. So don't feel like you need all the infrastructure of a website and everything that we have. You're looking, what you're seeing the two of us produce is very long down the line of the process. So don't measure against that, measure against, so where are you starting? And if you look out over 90 days, what would you feel comfortable having added based on where you are? And that might be a couple of pieces of content. It might be 10, but this is all arbitrary, it's all made up. You focus on the spirit of this particular module that we're talking about. If you focus on treating everybody who you interact with who comes into your world and raises their hand through their awareness and says, I'm kind of interested in what you're up to. If you treat that person like a customer, like a friend, that one decision has so many positive downstream consequences that it's impossible to fathom. That's why we call it a strategic upstream decision. The ripple effects of that one decision are profound. All right, this is- I just wanna add one extra piece to that because you mentioned something that's important. You mentioned the cognitive load and being respectful to people that are new to your world. And so in our context, if somebody stumbles on our website and browsers around, we're not being respectful if we produce this reams and reams and reams of deep stuff on any narrow piece or a narrow idea that's gonna require such a cognitive load to somebody brand new, that's gonna be scary for them and they're gonna disappear. So, you should be mindful of the fact that people entering in the world, that world needs to be entertaining, it needs to help solve a problem, be something that pulls them forward as opposed to it being scary. Whereas the next logical step for them is like, I like what these guys are doing and then they'll upturn and get onto the email list. A few years ago, maybe just before Sean joined, I decided to give away Autism Under Madness 2. So the entire course that people pay money for, but I only gave it away to people on the email list. So I sent an email, anybody interested, raise your hand and I'll send you the whole thing as it was presented when I first released it back in 2011. So it was delivered as emails. And that's like an 80,000 plus word course that people just have to raise their hand and they could have it for free, even though they weren't a customer. And I don't know, a little over 5,000 people went through Autism Under Madness 2 in that three month period for free. And many of them became customers of Autism Under Madness, even though they had the course for free. They loved that old course so much that it pulled them forward to want to be part of the current version, for example. And there's, but there's many more of them that decided not to become a customer yet. And they've kept hold of that course because it's valuable. But that only went to people that were on the email list because there was a relationship already established with them. I would never put that on the website as a free thing because, well, we could, but probably that's probably not the best place to put it because it's just too much. That's something of value that we could share with an audience. So I think that's just a, like a nuance. I'm gonna jump on to the next question because it's a world building. We have a bunch of world building questions and I know how much you love world building. So, all right, we're gonna do our time. All right, we're gonna start off with Joe. Joe says, I'm an email marketing agency owner. So most of my questions are oriented toward how to successfully implement this for their clients. I tried to frame the questions in a way that would still be helpful to non-agencies as well. All right, first question. For email marketing agencies doing this whole process for their clients, do you have any suggestions on how to approach world building as a collaborative effort or gather info from the client to effectively manage this? Should you simply go through the five step framework exercise with a client? I'll jump on this answer first and then hand it over to you, Andre, just because having worked with a million clients, this is one of the most miserable things in the world to do. You know, clients, for better or worse, clients want results. They want results in a very specific period of time, which is usually yesterday. One of the challenges with working with clients is that what we are describing is counterintuitive. Like the approach we're describing is counterintuitive, yet it's highly effective, but it's not, and it's based on reality, yet it's not a reality that's easily grasped. So when you try to sell this idea to a client, there is education involved in the client. There's trust required from the client. There are ways you can sort of stack the deck in your favor. So if I were going back to my agency days and decided I wanted to be, I was going to be an email marketing agency, I wanted to take, I wanted to use the art of email on behalf of clients and I needed to get their buy-in, including world building. One of the things I would figure out early is what two to three simple things do I need the client to accept and internalize, not just say they understand, but truly accept and internalize in order for them to be a good customer. So one of those things would be this reality that most people who buy are not going to buy early and that the signal that doesn't mean we're not going to attract buyers early. It means that the signal that we get early is indicative of a much larger signal over time and that we're not trying to hyper-optimize the tiny signal and we're not trying to, and we have to be very careful that we don't do things that, in the process of making that early result better, compromise where the real opportunity is later. And I would have that as part of every single conversation I had with the client, I would have that simple framework. What are we doing? What are we planning on doing right now? So that's the first question. What is the thing that we're talking about? How do we think specifically that thing is going to improve results in the first 30 days? Okay, that's the second question. And this has to be precise. Are there any downstream consequence of that beyond 90 days or 30 days, whatever it is, but is there something we're not thinking about that could cause problems later if we do this this way? Because what you're doing is your training, and this is what I spent endless amounts of time often unsuccessfully working with clients, training to train them to think like I think. And in order to get them to think like I think, I had to think out loud a lot. And I would say, and I would be very clear, this is how I think about it. If we accept that this is true, whatever most people who buy are going to buy in the first 90 days, or most people who buy aren't going to buy in the first 90 days, then if we believe that's true, then does this make sense? And often what you find is that what you're contemplating doesn't make sense. And if the client insists on making decisions that don't make sense, at some point you have to bring them to a decision point, which is, I would say this to clients all the time, my job with you is to produce, this is what I'm optimizing for, the long-term financial health of this enterprise. And I know that in order to do that, I have to focus the majority of my attention on where people are going, when people are likely to buy and that's after 90 days. So the decision you're asking me to make is a decision that's going to compromise why you've hired in the first place. You're asking me to undermine the results that you hired me for, and that doesn't seem optimal. I mean, choose your language, again, hyper-rational. That's the language I would use. And the shocking number of times the clients will do it anyway. And that's when you have to make a choice. That's one of the reasons I don't do client work anymore. There are only so many times you can bang your head against the wall before it starts to really hurt. So, you know, this is a very long answer to this question, but I want to give you a real answer, which is you're not going to get a client to sign off on a world building. It's the idea is to amorphous to a femoral, and they're just not going to get it. What you need to get them to sign off on is that you are an expert, that you understand, well, here's sort of the order you need to get them to sign off on, but you need to get 100% clarity on the actual result that they want. And if the result that they want is to make the cash registers ring within 30 days, and you explain to them that that's not really the opportunity, but they say, that's what we focus on anyway. Okay, well, then at that point, you're optimizing for something different and everything that follows changes. But if they hire you and say, hey, we want you to improve our, we've heard you're great at what you do, we want you to bring some of that greatness to our business, then that's when you have to sit down with them and say, okay, here's how I understand the reality of this situation. X number of people are buying from, whatever, here are your current results. You want to improve those results. In order to improve those results, we have to accept the reality that most people who are entering your world now, who are going to buy from you eventually are not going to do that in the first 90 days. That's reality. So knowing that we're going to do things that are designed to improve the likelihood, to increase the likelihood that people, when they do buy, or when they're likely to buy, will buy. And that's going to, and then you just go through the list. That will include things that we call world building, which, and you can kind of go through that list, but don't try to sell them on the method. Sell clients on the outcome and get them to restate back to you that your understanding of the outcome that they want is accurate. It's a shocking number of times when it's not. And for me, if I could identify the single most impactful mistake that I made as an agency owner, owning an agency for 22 years, it was that I was far too willing to assume that the client wanted the result that made the most sense to me, rather than spending the time to verify that the result they wanted, like for me to understand that the result that they wanted. So I would go in and I would assume that what they cared about was the long-term financial health in the business, when in fact, what they cared about was that their cost per lead was under $3, measured over 30 days. Like that, sometimes that's the reality. That is a very long, long, long, long question, or long answer. All right, Andre, I didn't tee that up for you very well. So any thoughts on how to manage clients? You don't work with clients, so your thought is always, I don't work with clients. No, I was just more broadly about world-building, I guess, I'll comment on that, that you don't have to do advanced world-building with multi-page presales that take people down little journeys and end with some call to action or no call to action. And creating these little narrative journeys, it can be simpler than that. What the Paris Review does, what the New Yorker does, and those articles that they have on their site that are accessible for free, that's a version of world-building. They're sharing long-form content with an audience that goes to them and seeks out world-class long-form content. So at no point are they trying to convince you or convince their audience to read long-form 5,000, 10,000 word articles. Those people show up anyway and they're just sharing stuff on the outside world and some of this stuff isn't accessible or you can get halfway through an article and then it's kind of grayed out and there's a nudge to come inside if you wanna see more. That's all versions of world-building that anybody can do. I think it's important to start off with what the audience is there for, what they expect to see and what they enjoy consuming. And then world-building kind of takes care of itself and you can go advanced or you can just keep it basic like they do. The next question I think is a short answer. So the next question, do you ever use customer development surveys, open-ended questions to collect qualitative data to perform world-building and email copywriting? The short answer is yes. We did it earlier this year. We sent out a, I think we talk about this in the value newsletter module too. We sent out, I think it's January-ish, an email. We had eight questions, scale of one to 10 and then we had an open-ended question. And we got hundreds and hundreds of replies. And we used that. Like we, a lot of the content that we created this summer and in the spring was content that we sort of, based on those results, we knew that it would resonate with our audience. There were some surprises in there. There was a lot of interest in the personal knowledge management stuff that we love so much. So that was, there was some surprises. The open-ended questions were phenomenal. When we created, I created the Momentum Builder Workshop, the first one for Andre's birthday. I relied very heavily on that data to, again, this, I don't know if everyone, what's it called, that was the story of that, but Andre sort of dropped that little bomb in my lap. Like, I don't know, on a Thursday, like, oh, hey, it's my birthday. And we usually do this thing and the idea is, and like an idiot, I say, oh yeah, sure, I'll take care of it. Don't worry, I mean, that's what friends do, right? Somebody's birthday, I want to give them a good present. So I had, I created the Momentum Builder Workshop sort of conceptually with some help from a few others, not Andre, but created that really based on looking through those survey responses and understanding like this is something that not everybody, but a segment of our audience really seems to be looking for this type of experience. So it was that world-building necessarily. I mean, the content we created certainly came from that. I think there is some danger, this to be, I said this is a short answer, I can't think short answers apparently. The only thing to be cautious of is if you send out a customer survey and then you get your answers and you only create based on what you've got for answers. Well, then, I mean, you're asking an audience who knows less about a topic than you do to tell you, probably, to tell you what they're interested in and then only creating that for them. Well, they're missing out on the richness of all the things you know that they don't know. Now, you can ask good questions and you can sort of elicit that and the way you ask your questions can make sure you don't fall into that trap. But just be careful that you as the creator have a role in this too. So you're using the results from a survey and from open-ended questions to inspire your thinking not to replace your thinking. Any thoughts on that question, Andre? Yeah, I mean, every single email, pretty much every single email that we ever send out is an opportunity for an open feedback loop. It's people hit respond and it goes into HelpScout and we get to read those emails. And many of the things that we create and send out, like Sean said, we know more most of the time about what we know. So we think that a certain idea will resonate and we write it up. We also use it as an opportunity just to unpack our thinking and then send it out. And it kind of builds on a concept or philosophy that Jim Collins shared in one of his books, Bullets vs Cannonballs. So us writing those emails, my very first systems thinking email that I sent out was me just unpacking my current understanding of the concept, putting it into email and clicking send. And that would either resonate or it wouldn't. And it's just, people responded to that in a way that was overwhelming. I mean, there were hundreds of people responded to that, to that email, which was a signal that, well, okay. So our audience resonate with this idea of systems thinking. So we turn that into a living article on the website that people can now find. And we double down on sharing this idea and also expanding our own understanding of how our business operates through the lens of systems thinking. So yeah, so that was all from just sitting out an email that a bullet, which then turned into a cannonball, which is now you can, we express the idea of systems theory all of our products, all of our stuff now. And that was as a result of sharing something. All right, I'll move on to the next question. So much I wanna say about that other question. I shut my mouth. All right, for brands that are already very successful using more traditional linear sales funnels, how do you transition them toward this approach without risking an immediate revenue drop off? For example, many of our clients spend bots on Facebook ads and recouping ad spend as a consideration in the short term, even though I understand it will pay off long term. So here's the short answer that the question is this, how do you transition clients toward the approach that we're advocating with a longer term time horizon without risking an immediate revenue drop off? The short answer is it is very difficult to be in a business where you're requiring your customers to change their minds. We as a species are not good at changing or we don't like to change our minds. So to go into a market and to say, everybody is trying to do things this, and I'm laughing my ass off as I say this because this is exactly what Andre and I are doing, but we're heretics or whatever. But to go into a market and say, the way you've been doing this and the way you want to do this isn't as good as the way that we do it and you should do it our way. That is a really, really steep hill to climb. Now you may have to because you already have clients. However, where I, the short answer is look for people who are already seeing problems with the way they're doing things. Because those people are far more receptive to doing things differently. Going into a business where they're doing things a certain way and they have a certain expectation return on investment in a certain period of time and then asking them to change their ways, knowing it may affect their short-term performance. You're setting yourself up for all kinds of problems. What you want to find are the clients who, they already sense that something's wrong. Like they're the ones complaining about it. Facebook ad costs are going through the roof and the quality of leads is dry, like whatever. We all have a typical complaints about traffic and we've got to figure out how we're going to make this better. Okay, well now they're receptive to change. Now that's kind of a half-assed answer. I'm like sneaking around the question you asked, actually asked, which is you already have clients. So consider that answer, the how do you get new clients where it's not quite so hard to do this? That's the question I actually answered. So now I answered the question you really asked. It comes down to, this doesn't have to be all or none. There are a few ways to approach this. One you can say, all right, traditionally, this is how you're monetizing your traffic now. Don't try to replace that, try to improve it. Take the principles that we teach, look at what your customers are doing and then recognize that those, the principles, something as simple as going to module two principles of serialized narratives, look at how they're engaging now and see how you could use principles of serialized narrative to capture and pull attention forward farther in. Like look for the leverage points. Let's just say for the sake of argument, I don't know your clients, but maybe they're doing traditional lead gen where they have an opt-in and there's a multi-part series that leads to a sale. Look at that series and ask yourself, is there more we could be doing in the emails that they're sending now to begin to establish more of a relationship, to begin to focus more and recognize empathy and then credibility before we move on to solution? Like just look for those little leverage points because the moment you show a client a positive change, when you go in and you say, okay, this is what you're doing, we're going to make this sort of simple three or four changes and we think this is going to happen, we're going to see this improvement and the improvement happens, you now have created the platform on which to go back to the client and say, now we would like to do this other thing because now you've earned their trust. And you, I have no doubt you already have the trust on your clients, but it's really a matter of how much are we asking our clients to do? How much risk are we asking our clients to take? I like minimizing that risk to say, we just want to try this one thing. You're already doing this, we think we can make this better. Let's go make that better. And it's like, okay, well, we've made that better. There's another way we can make it even better and maybe that way is more significant. You say, so what we're going to do is we want to compare. So we're going to take, half of your traffic, we're going to send them through this method and half we're going to send through this method in 30 days, we're going to evaluate results this way. Then you're minimizing their risk and there's just, there are lots of ways to thin slice this so that it's clear that you're not just coming in and saying, hey, I need you to trust that I know exactly what I'm doing. The results are going to be perfect for you and let's go do it and take a huge risk with me. Just bring the risk down from the client's perspective and showing them positive signals gives you the opportunity to build on that goodwill, build on those results. And maybe you find a sweet spot where what they're doing works for them and you can make it 20, 25% better and everybody's happy with 20 or 25% better in that scenario. We don't always have to get to 100%. Working with clients, it's a different animal. All right, Andra, any thoughts on this one? Yeah, I'll share a story then that's kind of relevant. A friend of mine, he used to work at one of the UK affiliates for Agora and he left and he got another job at a similar sort of place. And he was very, he had a deep understanding of sphere of influence and how the whole thing works. And when he, so he's in a new job now and he pitched his bosses, he said, give me some of the budget, like a small piece of the budget and let me build a very different looking thing. And just trust me, if it doesn't work, no harm if it works, well then we all can reap the rewards. And he built a sphere of influence inspired front end, which he did in parallel to the work he was meant to be doing for them. And it completely outperform everything that they've ever done before. So that was a fairly easy way for him to demonstrate that to them because he knew it would work, they didn't. So he just asked them nicely to give him a small budget and he built this thing out in parallel and it performed better than anything's ever performed for this company. So suddenly he was a superhero and that's all he does for them. I'm not sure if he still works anymore, but that's that story. So I think that's a simpleish way of doing it. You just run something in parallel and it either works or it doesn't. Yeah, that's the fundamental insight with clients, right? Results speak for themselves. You get a client a better result, you're a hero. That's just it, but getting them there can be the challenge. All right, let's move on to Denise. Denise's question is, when thinking about creating content to build worlds, how do you decide what's public for everyone to see and what's for people who enter the funnel? Laughing at this because we don't do funnels. I also understand that I need to start with a simple idea of a world and allow myself the time and space to continue to add to it. So I'm just wondering, what does that look like? Do I need to imagine the path someone would take, draw a line, use that as my starting point? For example, start with the assumptions people have, create enough tensions so people can learn. You can see that maybe they are missing something, invite them to learn more. I love that last paragraph. I'll tackle this first in the hand of Steve. That seems to be working for the moment. So how do we decide what's public for everyone to see and what's for people who enter the funnel? We don't have funnels, so I guess some of them might say that we do conceptualize it that way. We don't think about it that way. We don't think about, we've mentioned this earlier. We don't bifurcate between inside the tent, outside the tent, very succinctly. I mean, mostly for us, it's depth, right? You know, the art of email will probably end up somewhere in the neighborhood, I don't know, 100,000 words plus with a spoken word and the written word, you know, evolution over time, all, you know, transcripts, so 150,000 words. Like that's, we're not handing out 150,000 words in an email, that's just way too much. Way too much to expect of people, way too much intellectual overhead and cognitive load, especially for people who are not interested in the art of email marketing. It would be, we would never do that because just for them, it would make sense. So the initial bifurcation is what does the audience want? And you know, 1,500, 2,000 words, email and in articles for everybody who can self-select, they can choose to read it, choose not to read it. That kind of makes sense, that might be the upper end, you know, maybe we're creating a little, certainly when we went to weekly emails this summer, there were times where I felt like we're probably asking too much of our audience. So that's a balance, we talked about that in the art of email in different places, that finding that balance. But there's no real checklist really for you, I think where you answered the question, or the way you asked the question in the last paragraph, I think is really it. And you said, for example, start with the assumptions people have, create enough tension so people can see that maybe they are missing something, invite them to learn more, I love that. The thing to keep in mind is that people who are moving toward you are interested in knowing what you know. And your first job is to figure out what do you know that's valuable to them? And then your next job is to figure out in what order is that valuable so that it makes sense to them. And you put what you know in some sort of context. And that context is focused on empathy because you have to ask yourself, what do I think based on how this person has entered my world, how do I think I can be helpful for them? Well, if they're interested in X, then like for example, we use the art of email, if someone's interested in email marketing, and they get to the manifesto, the first thing that we say in the manifesto is that, we think conventional wisdom steers you wrong, but it's not obvious how it steers you wrong. Like you can, the moment you accept a flawed idea, everything that you do after that makes sense, until you realize that the idea that you accepted is flawed. And once you recognize that that idea is flawed, every decision you make after changes. So here's the flaw, here's how you should think about it instead. Like that's all education because the moment someone gets to the end of that, they now see the world, the way we see the world, and then we can take them systematically on more of a journey to say like, okay, now if you understand that, the next thing to talk about is this. And once you understand that, then we talk about the next thing. And it's really, it really is building out, I don't, I think of it in like concentric circles, where you're starting from like a core idea, and then you're layering on and layering on. And at some point, somebody wants a lot more, and that generally becomes the point at which there's an offer, there's the exchange of payment versus, and that's different for you and us and everybody on this call, like when does that point happen? But it's not a hard line, it's a fuzzy line. And the most important resource your audience has is their attention. It's the one thing we can't get back. We can always make more money. We can get everything else that we need, except more time. So when someone gives you their attention, that is the strongest imaginable signal, far more powerful than someone just giving you money. I bought a million things I didn't pay attention to. But if you have my attention, everything's different. So we're really engineering that front end around the capturing, the earning, and the ongoing earning of an audience's attention. And the point at which revenue comes from that, probably will reveal itself organically, I think. That's gonna seem kind of a weird answer, but all right, Andre, give Denise a better answer than that. Well, okay, so I'll use the Sphere of Influence front end that are created back in 2018. I've just dropped the link into the thing. So as you suggested in your question, is that you can start small. And Sphere of Influence used to have its own website. And literally on that website could have been what you see on that link, which is the product launch marketing manifesto that I've written. It's like it's 10 pages long, and people have to go through it. And so that entire world, that website could have been just those 10 pages. It's a 10 page website, and it pulls people forward through this narrative.
