So earlier I said thank you to Mary, so thanks Mary. Apparently I can't figure out how Zoom works, so thank you for you to actually see it. Okay, next question. You wanna say something? Sorry, all right. Next, we have so many questions. I thought we'd just gonna start moving along, but this is, we don't wanna rush this, right? I mean, these are nuanced questions. If we're gonna take the time to ask questions, we wanna take the time to answer them. So we appreciate you're bearing with us in this. All right, so this is Anthony. This he's quoting us, we've created a world for our ideal audience to explore at their pace, not ours. No optimized funnels, no false scarcity, no unnecessary urgency. Okay, this is the question. Does that mean no encouragement for email opt-in either, and just leave it completely up to your audience? What about a soft opt-in invitation without a tripwire? Basically, should I make the email opt-in super obvious to them at the end of each and every valuable piece of content or a sidebar? Actively encourage them to opt-in or just wait for it to happen passively? That's the first question. So nothing, no, absolutely not. Sure, I mean, you can encourage people to opt-in, but in what context, right? I mean, because the context in which you ask somebody to opt-in influences the, that opt-in, influences the initial experience and quality of that lead. So if you say, you know, here's like one page over, you know, like here's kind of who we are. If you like us, sign up here and you'll get this free report. We created about 50 ways to improve your lead generation. Okay, well, you're going to get people who want, I mean, now you're going to get a muddy response. Some people who opted in, read what you wrote and loved it, and they're like, these are my people. I finally found them. And then some percentage of the people who opt-in are going to opt-in because they want the bright and shiny thing. Now, is that good or bad? It just, it just is. So you're going to have an email audience made up of two sub audiences, a group that just resonated with every word that you spoke and a group that wanted the free shiny thing. And my guess is based on my experience that the quality of the free shiny thing crowd is going to be lower than the people who just raise their hand. And so this sounds right to me. So we're not saying don't encourage people to opt-in. Encourage the hell out of it. But what we think, the way we think you're encouraging them is not saying, here's something really interesting. And if you want something even more interesting, guess what? You have to give me your email address. Well, it's transactional, right? And it's not good or bad. It just creates an effect. And the question is, do you want to have that effect in your business? There's no right answer. There's just your answer. We don't want that effect in our business. Doesn't mean we don't ask for the opt-in. If you go to any of the content on our site, it says at the top, this was published in our emergent marketing newsletter. If you are interested, you can subscribe here. And absolutely, we're saying they can. But we're not saying, here's 500 words about XYZ, a simple testing framework. And if you'd like to read the remaining thousand, then you need to give us your email address. That changes the nature of the interaction, all of a sudden. Is it good? Is it bad? It's just different. What we're saying, what we're trying to say, is be aware that what you're doing has consequences and that you are then subject to the consequences for a very long period of time. So if you buy 250 leads, because you've promised them 1,000 free, bright and shiny things, you're going to have a lot of people, you're going to have comparatively more people on your email list of lower quality, all things being equal. And there's nothing wrong with that. Their entire businesses, and I've worked with many of them, that have enormous email lists that on balance produce good results. And there's nothing that says that's wrong. But from our perspective, particularly because we're buying traffic, we're buying attention, when we pay for attention, we want the attention that we pay for to be of a certain quality so that it's worthwhile for us. And we're willing to pay a lot more for it. That's just the decision that we've made. Every day we sit down to a buffet of consequences of our actions. And traffic is one of those areas, how you get a lead has consequences. And you just be aware of them. That's all we're suggesting, because you will be subject to their effects. And subject to understanding those effects as you improve your business. That's all. That's how I think about it. Yeah. Yeah, I mean, I'll just reiterate what you said, that opt-ins, everything leading up to an opt-in is very intentional. Definitely wouldn't stick opt-ins on the sidebars. In a sidebar where there's no context, definitely wouldn't do exit pops. I think that's done to get somebody on to an email list. Maybe an exit pop that talks about a recipe that's awesome, like a new recipe, and it leads to the recipe. But then maybe at the end of the recipe could be an opt-in. But popping up for opt-ins and sidebar opt-ins, that's, I don't think that's the way to go for the reasons that Sean's already articulated. All right, and Anthony asked a second. We got this question so many different ways. I'm seeing it, it seems like a thousand different places, so I'm really excited that we get to answer, I hope answer this question. So he quoted us again, an insight is not just helpful information. So here's the question, could you give us an example or two? I understand an insight is certainly on a higher level than helpful information, but what's the level above insights? If we give away insights for free, what's the thing that's even better and customers really need to pay to acquire? I thought about this one, I'll jump in first. Okay, I think I made this up, I think I've since heard other versions of this, so maybe I didn't make it up and I think I did, but it doesn't really matter. So here's how I, when I used to teach Google Analytics, primarily to clients so they could figure out what they were looking at. And I would teach analytics in a three-part framework, data, information and insights. Data is just when you see a number, a thousand people came to the website this month, that's data. Information is when that there's some context for that. So a thousand people came to the site this month, but the same month last year, 500 people came to the website. Okay, well that's information, right? 500 more people came, we doubled the number of people year over year who came to our website in this particular month, that's information. And then where insight happens is when you can explain why that thing happened in a way that's actionable or informative. So we might look and say like, oh, okay, we had 500 more people came to the site this year. And then you realize, well, that's not meaningful because it was as goofy bot traffic from some Russian bot farm, so that doesn't really mean anything. Well, that's not an insight, but you might look and see, well, this article that we published two years ago just got a bunch of organic search traffic and our website didn't get more traffic. This one article got a lot more traffic. And then you start to ask yourself, I wonder why did that resonate with, now you're getting to the level of something meaningful, that might guide action, like maybe we should expand on that or maybe we should do, that should be the focus of some lead generation because that seems to have attracted some attention. So that's kind of the framework. So here are some examples that we've shared. I'm gonna do two examples. The first one is, this is information we've shared with our audience, which is that over the 40 plus years that Andre and I have been in this business, we've never seen a causal relationship between a tactic and an outcome. There's no, we've never seen a particular funnel, particular method, a particular thing that says that this thing guarantees this result. That's just information. When we took it to the next level though, we said, because of that observation, the way we approach marketing is from the perspective of stacked probabilities. So that we, even though we know there's no specific thing that guarantees results, we have observed lots of decisions that in total can create better results. Well, that's an insight. The insight is like, oh, okay, I'm not in the looking for the magic bullet business. I'm in the avoiding mistakes and making better decisions business. The person who internalizes that, a lot changes for that person because the person who gets the aha moment from that, that insight that, oh, I don't have to go out and constantly be on the lookout for the one thing that's going to change my business. I just need to pay attention to the things that each one gives me, it sort of stacks the deck in my favor and that in aggregate, that improves my business. That's an insight somebody would get from it. That's a distinction. Second example we've used already in this conversation many times, this one I think is more obvious. Dean Jackson's observation that 15% of people who buy within two years, buy in the first 90 days, that is just information. That's just, it's a piece of information. But when you realize that there's four times, the financial opportunity in an audience after 90 days, and you then change everything about how you do business, because of that observation, that's an insight. In many ways the art of email in its entirety is an insight based on the realization that relationships matter. That's at its core. Some people might say, oh, that's just information. Okay, we can split hairs all day long with this. But the thing that someone can, the aha moment someone can have that they can act on because of a different understanding of the world that they've internalized, that to me I think is a pretty decent example of our definition of an insight. Andre, what are your thoughts on this one? Yeah, I can't better that. I mean, if you've nailed it. In fact, right on module one, customers are credited before they buy anything. That's an insight. That's, you know, it says everything. And yeah, so that seems to be one of our most popular upstream, strategic upstream decisions because it's such a powerful insight. And once you hear it, once it's been unpacked and you internalize it, you can't unhear it. And then suddenly it just shows up everywhere, which is very different to just a piece of information. One of the next questions is from Amanda. I'm keen to hear any book recommendations you have around world building to add to my morning reading routine. I'll let you start on this one. Andre, I have really one. Yes. Yeah. The caveat with world building is it can be complicated. It can't seem complicated and it's easy to- The master of understatement. I love that. It can be very overwhelming. I mean, you can, there is, there are world building, there's a world building, well, it's partially a world building masterclass on masterclass.com. You know, that's mainly for people that are writing fiction, but you can use those as takeaways. One that I've seen more recently, in fact, it was unpacked this year by a employee of Spotify. So I'll just post the link inside of the chat floor. And I've made sure that I've selected everyone. But the word of warning is this is deep, deep, deep. And it's not ideal for most people because it'll just be too overwhelming. But you know, Amanda, you've asked so read that. It's like a, I don't know, it's maybe a five part long form essay. Well, it's five essays that make up this idea of world building as expressed by Alex Denko, who's an employee of Spotify. He's a smart guy and he's done a lot of research on that, on what world building means in the business at Spotify, what they do, what he does for them. And he's just basically used this, his website as an opportunity to share his thinking on it and to better understand what world building means. So it's a very deep, deep article. So just be careful when you read that. And so my recommendation for world building first would be from, you asked for books. So I would read several of Michael Lewis's books. I would start with a premonition. And it's his book about sort of the lead up to COVID-19, the pandemic and sort of this very small group of people who had they not done what they did in a very unofficial, largely obstructed capacity, probably millions more people in the United States would have died. I mean, it's a profound story. I would also read the Undoing Project Michael Lewis's book, really anything by Michael Lewis, the high speed trading when that was a good one. But the reason I suggest Michael Lewis is because first of all, it's narrative nonfiction. I mean, he's writing about real things, real people, incredibly interesting. But he builds worlds in ways that are, I mean, he builds worlds that we can understand from almost the moment you start reading the book. Like he is putting you in, he's putting you in real places with real people who are really interesting, communicating oftentimes complex ideas that coalesce. The characters are parts of a larger whole and throughout the telling of his stories, the larger whole reveals itself, sometimes explicitly, often explicitly, sometimes implicitly. But just do the power of the written word in really good characters. He creates worlds that the reader inhabits so thoroughly. I can't read as, I can't read a Michael Lewis book after about noon because I dream about it all night, everything. Like I inhabit that world. So, and he's an incredible writer as well. So I would read, I think I would start with a premonition. There are a handful of characters. The Undoing Project is just wonderful, just ridiculously wonderful. But read a few of those and just, and I go back, I go back and I look and see how I will go read the chapters. What the hell did he do here? When he first introduced a character, how did he introduce the character? Why by the end of that chapter do I feel like I know this character and I really understand her and have empathy for her and I'm interested in what happens next. And I sense something big is coming. I sense she fits into a larger whole. How the hell does he do that? And then just go, the challenge with Michael Lewis's books is they're so good that you have to read them cover to cover. And, but then you can, don't try to, like for me, I wouldn't want to deconstruct it on the first experience. Read it, experience it, go back, and then dissect like what the hell is he, what has he done? And then the shorter answer, it's not a book answer. This is a question. I love questions. When you're thinking about world building, the one question to ask yourself all the time is what else? That's the question. So, you've created something, whatever, you've got a landing page, what else? Well, once people, you may, if you're doing a sphere of influence inspired front end, you might have this coalescing belief that you want someone to accept before they're ready to opt in the right people and that maybe you have to make four or five points before you get there. So you kind of figure that out. But now they've opted in, they've raised their hand, they said, I'm the right person, well, what else? Well, now they need to understand that, okay, what else? Well, then they need to understand this, great. And eventually when you answer what else, five or six times, you've got a relationship building series. Well, great, well, what else do we need? Well, there's this concept that, it's really three parts, great. Start creating the three parts. And once those are done, and now you have the three pieces of content that are sort of publicly visible in a relationship building series and often, well, what else? Well, now we're sort of thinking maybe I want people to have a sense, because my audience, where's my audience relative to purchasing something from me? Okay, we'll start. Maybe you have a soap opera series where you're leading to some sort of hand raising, okay? Great, well, what else? It's always, world building is always the answer to the question, what else? And you never, ever, ever stop answering that because every time you create something new, and this is the fundamental, most profound insight of the art of email, that I don't know that we've articulated well yet, because it's hard to articulate in the written word, much easier to articulate in the spoken word, which is at the center of the art of email is this profound idea that the moment you introduce one new thing into the system, the entire system changes. It is not linear additive. You're like, oh, I have a landing page with seven emails, and so I have an eight part system, and when I add the next thing, it's one more layer, it's now nine parts. That's not how systems theory works. The math, and we talked about in the art of email series, the enrollment series about how we had rewritten one particular email over and over again, I wrote the base for the email that was just ridiculously bad. It was bad, not because the idea was bad, it was the expression of the idea was bad, which is, this is what I was trying to share, which is in systems theory tells us it's not the parts, it's the relationship among the parts, and anytime you add a new part, you change the nature of those relationships. The example I used is I worked with a well-known internet marketer for a period of time who scaled his business from four people to 16 people in one shot, and in his mind, it failed miserably. And the reason it failed was because conceptually, we think that a 16 person business is four times more complex than a four person business, was 16, four times greater than four, right? But a systems theory tells us that a 16 person business is 20 times more complex than a four person business because of the number of potential relationships among the people. When you have a three person business, you have four people who have relationships with three other people, right? That's how it works. When you have a 16 person business, you have 12 people who have relationships, I'm sorry, you have 16 people who have relationships with 15 other people. The math gets crazy. So bringing this back to the art of email, when you're world building, every time you introduce one new piece of content into the system, the entire system changes because there's a new relationship in the system. So if you have initially landing page, seven part relationship building series to value newsletter, that's not a very complex system, but the moment your value newsletter, you create a new piece of content by asking and answering what else, the whole system has changed. Is now you have this other entry point into the system. But this is such a profound idea that it's hard to capture in words, but this is at the core of the power. You get exponential, you have the potential for exponential benefit with the art of email because the entire thing is built to work together as a system. I didn't need to talk about that, but. Okay, let's move on to the next thing. This one has tremendous amounts of background. So this is about world building. The question really, so this is focusing on, the question is for the politics part of world building when considering who has power in the market and how it's exercised and displayed. I'm uncertain what to consider as my market. Now this is, I'm gonna summarize what Matt has shared with us here because it's a lot. Matt is in a, his market is located, it's a geographic place, Alaska. He is, his audience are people who are interested, my sense is that particular place. He has a website with photojournal, he wants to sell photos, visit consultations like people who are coming there already. You know, I'm just looking through this to make sure I cover the basics so we all understand it. I think that's most of it. So his question really is when you have this hyper local, no real competitors doing what he's doing, how do you think about politics? Is this idea in world building? Because politics focuses on who has power in the market and how that power is exercised and displayed. I'm gonna give a very short answer to this question and then Andrea will hand it over to you. But the short answer to this question is, that part of world building is not relevant to what you're doing. That's a simple answer. So skip it. Politics, we included that in our conception of world building because it's impossible to think about our prospects outside of the context of other players in the market. So Andrea and I have consulted with some of the larger financial publishing companies. When you work in that market, you can't forget that overwhelmingly people in that market are sold to a very specific way. What they see in their inbox in front of them every day has a certain character to it. And when we think about how we want to approach that market, we don't do it in a vacuum. We have to think about it in the context of what they're seeing all day, every day, knowing that influences what we're showing them. So that's why politics matters. Like for us, lots of people in our market talk about the created language around funnels and tripwires. And we can't pretend that that language doesn't exist. We can't pretend that our audience isn't exposed to that. So for us, politics is really recognizing. Like that's out there, that's real. And we need to be aware of it so that when we are engaging with our audience, we're recognizing that the world that they are in includes some things that we need to speak to, even though we don't believe in them, recommend them, etc. Anything to add on that, Andre? That's kind of a weird question, hard question. Yeah, it's a hard one. I mean, I'll add that you can also think laterally about this. Somebody that's going to go to Sipka, Alaska, is a traveler, presumably, somebody that likes a certain type of scenery or place. Well, I'm pretty sure that somebody that visits your little city in Alaska has visited other cities and other countries, right? So as a traveler, what is the market saying in doing, these people that travel to your neck of the woods or would like to travel to your neck of the woods, where else do they go? How are those people presenting their stuff? What are they saying? What are they doing? So you can glean some insights from that and then use that to inform some decisions about your little place in the world. That's anything I can add. Next question is about a couple more about world building as well. So first one, how do you know it's time to go from a town to a city and what does that process look like? I wouldn't conceptualize it that way. We're putting artificial names on a thing with a comfortable metaphor that we understand conceptually the difference in size between a town and a city, right? But it's not, so the way the question's written, that's not, don't think about it that way. Just sit down and create one piece of content, write a paragraph, write a sentence, whatever, and that sentence will turn into something else and use the what else framework or whatever. Just create your content and you'll wake up one day and you go, oh, this is, you know, there's a lot here. Maybe that's a town, maybe somebody else would think it's a village, maybe someone else thinks it's a metropolis. Who cares? Just create. I just, I don't see a lot of value in thinking about the process of going from one size to the other. Maybe I'm missing the point of the question. Andre, do you have a thought on that? I feel like I'm not seeing where that would lead to a valuable insight. I mean, it may just be missing that. Well, I think the reason why we expressed it that way is someone that's not doing any world building or they're just starting out and they don't have any world as such, maybe, but one of those final things. So it's easier to start small. So that could be a little three page, multi-page thing that people go down this little journey. That's three pages deep. Well, that's part of world building and it's a very small one. But when you start by looking at something that's huge with hundreds of pages and ask yourself the question, well, how can I build that? That's not productive. You get there by expanding something that starts small. That's all people expressing in that idea. I'm gonna move on to the next question. This is from Guillermo as well. Do you have a template framework for writing a manifesto? Why don't you answer this? I'm curious how you would answer this before I start running my mouth. The short answer is no, other than a framework which I think we may have shared before. I'm just trying to think now if we have, but it's really just how to think about deciding on what's gonna be inside of any long form piece. So I'll have to check, but you know, actually we definitely have spoken about it inside of the momentum building workshop as part of writing a landing page. We also have a simple writing framework. Yeah, that's right, which is always hard. Which has parts of it, right? Yeah, that's it. I mean, I think that the other answer to this, I'm tempted to just, I'm glad I waited for you to answer it because I was just gonna be like, no, move on, right? Which isn't a great answer. Only because I hate templates. It's this idea of a template makes me wanna scratch my eyes out. To think about a manifesto, here's a better answer. To think about a manifesto begin, well, here's a caveat. I write everything like a prosecutor. I'm always making a case. I mean, the lead, you can sort of see where I've opened, in the writing that we do, you can kind of see where I show up once in a while when it feels a little hard edge because that's just how my brain thinks. This is, I'm taking you on a journey and at the end of that journey, this is what I want you to believe. And in order to believe that, then I need to make the case for it. And I make the case in a way that to me feels consistent and rational and logical and step by step. I don't know how to do it a different way. So I give you that context, you almost so that you know when I answer this question, I'm answering only from my perspective. In a way, I think about writing a manifesto is that there is something at the end of that manifesto I want you to believe that it is the controlling idea that I want you to understand, internalize and accept if you're the right person, right? The right person meaning your high quality prospect or whatever. It's usually a sentence, that it's not 10 paragraphs because then that's what's the point, it is a thing, right? So that controlling idea, in order for that to be true, I then ask myself, what do you need to believe in what order so that this thing that I want you to accept at the end is the logical conclusion of the case that I've made, right? Now, how I make that case may not be that cold and precise, but when you get there, you get to the end, I want to have a very clear idea of what I want you to believe at that point. And I want to have done the work to have made an airtight case so that when you get there, you're nodding your head. If you get all the way to the end, then you've accepted each of the things that I've said that you're nodding your heads and this makes sense and it coalesces to this point where you go, yep, that's it. That is exactly it. Let's do a quick shout out for Amanda who's on this call, Amanda Bond. So Amanda has probably one of the best sphere of influence front ends I've seen. I don't even know if I would call it, I just, I'm thinking about your Facebook ad. So maybe it's not a traditional sphere of influence front end, but and I don't even know if it still exists, Amanda, if it does, I don't know if you want to share a link to it, if it doesn't, sorry for opening a loop for everybody who can't see something, but Amanda had this really great, she made such a compelling case for why the way most people were doing Facebook advertising was just didn't make any sense. The results sucked on and on and on and on, but she made such a compelling case that by the end of it, if you reach the end of it, all you wanted to do was figure out what should I do now? Because it just coalesced around this point that you couldn't help but share her worldview when you got there. It was brilliant. It's so, so brilliant. I had such a perfect example. That's the feeling that you want. It's hard. There are other examples too, and just seeing that was Amanda's on the call, I really want to point that out as I'm thinking about that, that that's the feeling that you want with a manifesto. And again, lower case manifesto, manifesto is just, it's just us sharing our opinions about some. You know, the manifesto for the art of email is longer, it's more involved and it's 2,200 words or so, where we do, we did a couple of interesting things with that, where we have characters who we reintroduce at a different point that are metaphorically related to the larger point. So we did a couple of things there that were nuanced, but at the core of that manifesto was a controlling idea, and that by the time you get to the end of it, we want you to have internalized that controlling idea. And we want you to feel like we made the case, an airtight case, that when you get to the end and we say something, you don't know, wait a minute, where the hell did that come from? That what we have said at the end is, it feels like right, of course, that's what an insight feels like, is when you sit back from your chair and you go, damn it, of course, that's why I haven't, getting the results I've been trying to get, because I've been doing all of these things that make perfect sense if this is true, but damn it, that's not true. So like, that's an insight, damn, of course. So go, Amanda, Shed to link, go read that, that Amanda created, and you'll feel, you'll feel what insight feels like, and you'll also feel what a really good manifesto, cap and lowercase manifesto feels like. All right, I'm gonna move on to the next question. Do you, you answered that one first. All right, next question from Guillermo. This is about world building. The last question about world building, shockingly. Would you say that second order thinking must be present in every step or only a focus in a bridging now? I cannot wait to, actually, this we're talking about bridging those now. We're not gonna answer this one. All right, so this is the bridging now upcoming. I'll go ahead and answer it, Andre. I know. Those I think out of brain damage. Yeah, I mean, second, third, fourth order thinking should be your default stance. You know, the world doesn't operate on first order thinking, in my opinion. I mean, everybody does it. It doesn't mean it's the right way to look at the world. You know, the world is complex. There's consequences to every single decision and consequences to those decisions. And it can go on and on and on. And every single decision that you've made has been a result of something that you've been exposed to that's mostly probably subconscious and other things that are conscious that you've just forgotten about and it's all affecting things. So yeah, we all need to think in second and third and fourth order. That's my answer too. I mean, there's thinking should be second order and beyond always. First order thinking doesn't make any sense. We need to go through first order thinking to get to everything else. But every decision that we make, we need to think about the second, third, fourth order consequences. We can get too analytical with this. We don't want to do that. But we think again, it gets back to some of the examples we shared earlier. If you give away free stuff to get leads, you have leads that like free stuff. No different than if you use Facebook's algorithm to get leads, you're getting people who generally sign up for stuff. Just be aware of the second order consequences of where you get your traffic, how you get your traffic. But all of that has consequences. It's not good or bad. It just is in second order thinking helps us better understand what is. That's all. All right, wow, we're making progress. We're all right with strategy module. Okay, this was a fun one. So the next question was often the visions we hear about it sound a lot more tangible and measurable, such as the founder had a vision ten years ago, blah, blah. So the question is, I guess, visions don't have to be tangible and measurable. Well, I'll answer this one first. Your vision can be whatever you want your vision to be. And if it resonates with you, that's all that matters. If if the thing, you know, the visions, if we want to think about this in a functional term, a vision creates contrast and contrast creates tension. And that tension pulls us forward. Right. So there's contrast between the vision you have for a desired future and where you are relative to that vision right now. And if that vision is attractive, it the idea is that that structural tension and Robert Fritz's work around structural tension, that future reality is so exciting, it pulls you toward it. If that's true, then the only vision that matters is the matter, the vision that free that lights you up and creates enough contrast that it pulls you or creates enough tension that it pulls you toward it. I'll give a personal example. I don't know if this is meaningful to anybody else. I have a strange relationship with money. For me, I am not motivated by money. I don't I don't sit down every day and look at sales and I don't do that. I'm not motivated by money. My relationship with money is that I want to make enough money that I don't have to think about money. Right. Is that a vision? No, I never said I don't have a vision board in my office where there's a picture of me and like a thought bubble and it has money in it. And there's like a red circle and an X. Like I don't I don't that's not part of my vision. But internally, I know that when I look out at a future and I think about the success of that, an ingredient in that is I don't want to think about money day to day, month to month. I just don't want it to be on my radar. I don't enjoy thinking about that. That's not a very compelling vision. It's not tangible. It's not measurable. I mean, how do you measure not thinking about money? I don't know. So I think to me, it just returns back to the central idea that I don't know that somebody else can help you or that somebody else's definition of the vision is a good fit. I mean, maybe for you, you know, you might look at this and think to yourself, you know what, 10 years from now, this business that I'm creating and a lot of people do this. I want to create this business that impacts 100,000 or more people through XYZ and make 100 million dollars out of it. And if you see that every day and that motivates you and that lights you up in your world is better as a result, hell, yeah, man, run with that all day long. So don't let anybody, especially us, suggest that that that our vision should be your vision or how we think about vision should be your vision. Now you you you find what lights you up and chase that every single day. That's like that's the best answer I can give to that question. Yeah, Andre, vision, tangible, measurable. Andre has a vision board, I bet. Do don't. I don't. Yeah. I don't think that many businesses start off thinking that they're going to build a billion dollar thing. First of all, we're writers, not copywriters. Never said you shouldn't be a copywriter. If copywriting lights you up, if you get a positive effect from the principles of copywriting, sure, I'm going to use them. Just be aware, this is where we get the second order consequences. Copywriting is designed for one specific purpose, which is to get people to buy. That's that's at the core of copywriting. So when you are get when you use principles of copywriting, you are using principles that are engineered to get people to buy right now. That's that's that's just that's not opinion. That's fact. That's what Claude Hopkins and Eugene Schwartz did. And olderly than many others. And that's what copywriting is about at its core, is how to take someone from prospect to customer and the traditional definitions of the term. We never say don't do that. But this idea and this is the point that we were making that if the only way you know how to communicate with an audience is to do with a single lens of copywriting, then you have this man with a hammer person with a hammer syndrome, which the adage is that's the law of the instrument, which is to a man with a hammer, everything looks like a nail. Better phrase to person with a hammer, everything looks like a nail. If you if you embrace copywriting as the way that you write and copywriting is a style of writing to get people to buy, everything that you write using the principles of copywriting will sound like you're trying to get people to buy. That's just a logical conclusion of that. And if when we say that overwhelmingly, we think there's power in building relationships first, copywriting is not a method of writing that builds relationships. It's a method of writing that creates sales. So we think there's a better way to write to build relationships, more conversational, more natural, more accessible to our audience. And sure, I mean, if you want to use principles of copywriting when it's time to sell, if that works for you, sure, some of our best friends are copywriters. I'm going to spend all after Barry is still on the call. Barry and I are going to spend all afternoon with some of the truly some of the best copywriters in the world. There's nothing wrong with copywriting. It's just not the only way to write. And if, you know, for us, 15% of the content we create has any relationship to selling. So to have all of our writing influenced by a copywriting principle, it just wouldn't make any sense for us. It wouldn't make any sense for the way we teach either. So long answer, short question. But there's nothing inherently wrong with copywriting, just like there's nothing inherently wrong with a sledgehammer. But if you're hanging a picture in your living room, probably don't want to use a sledgehammer. All right, Andre, take it away. Like what's, how do we come at this and answer the question at the same time in a way that's valuable to everybody? Yeah, I don't know. I think the, the meta idea is that there is no one way that's going to, you know, lifestyle or writing, at least how I, how I start to write is very different to Sean's. He's, he's ready. Well, knowledge on an express how he does it. But for me, I'm a discovery writer. So the very first thing I do is I need to, you know, write what I call a draft zero, which is just me getting the stuff out of my head. And then once I've done that, I can then create my, my, my, my first shitty first draft and I'll then take the ideas that are, you know, that reveal themselves on the page from my draft zero and I'll start to construct something that's, that's more, you know, that, that has more structure, but I don't worry about anything to do with structure when I'm just vomiting my draft zero onto a page. And that's just what I need to do to get the, the stuff out of my head. Yeah. Otherwise it just rattles around inside there. And then I do the shitty first draft and then shortly after that, I'll edit it to something that I'm happy with. And that doesn't have to be a long process for me. So before Sean came along and I had to do this all myself, some of the emails I wrote or the stuff I wrote was just a one version thing. Just, it just came out. It was more or less the way it needed to be. I felt and I would just do some, some spell checking, still get that wrong and then send it out. Whereas now Sean and I approach this slightly differently. So sometimes when, when structures may be more important initially, Sean will write a lead or he'll, he'll start it off. I'll still write my, my draft zero on the side just so I can express my ideas onto a page. I don't have to share that. And then I'll then edit what Sean gives me or vice versa. I'll send Sean a version that I've written, which is normally one order deeper than my draft zero. And then he will then look at it and sing and, and say to himself, well, this, this, this doesn't make sense. And he will restructure it and make it nice. Yeah. That's my answer. I mean, probably the most shocking thing in our relationship is two people who write as differently as we do write as seamlessly as we do together. It doesn't make sense. It shouldn't make sense. I think it speaks to this, speaks to two things. One shared values sort of make everything else more effective, but also there, there's no right way to write. It's adult. We're, we write so differently that the idea that there's a way to do this is insane. So the way I describe our two writing styles is Andre generally writes to find a lead. I write from a lead. So I have to figure out the very much. He, I say, he's Anne Lamont. I'm John McPhee. Right. So I have to figure out the structure. First, this idea that I've tried, like, you're just, you know, right by the seat of my pants, shitty first draft. I'd rather kill myself than ever do that. I hate it. Hate it. For me, I have to write a lead and that lead has to be a certain level of precision. It's my foothold into the writing. And then I write and what I will do is I feel like I'm running, running ahead, I don't know, 100 yards. And then at a certain point, my energy begins to diminish. And I go back to the beginning and I read what I've written already, edit slightly. And that propels me through the next 100 yards. And then, and I build a piece that way. I read and reread and edit. I also, after graduate school, I was a journalist for a year. So of course, as a journalist, you write from lead. You have to, the phrase don't bury the lead comes from journalism. Right. So for me, like, often I will, I will read the base piece that Andre has written. And I will find that he, he got the lead, usually between 50 and 60 percent of the way there. Andre, the lead for what Andre wrote has revealed itself in his writing. And then, but for me, I couldn't have written the first 60 percent or 40 percent without knowing what the lead is. So very different styles of writing. So specifically to answer your questions, how much editing do we do a lot? We edit very differently. I edit in tandem with my right. There's no, for me, editing and writing are not separate processes. There's one process for Andre. They're separate processes. Is one better, one worse? Nope. They're better or worse for each of us. Do we have a checklist we use? No. Well, maybe we have an unconscious checklist in our minds, was we've been writing for decades each. But we don't have, there is no checklist. It's like, OK, open loop check, cliffhanger check, PS check. We use none of that. There's no nothing to suggest that there's anything wrong with that. And certainly if that, that framework, I could see that being a very effective framework, like pulling ideas out of principles of serialized narrative and having sort of a checklist of things. Like, have I done these things? Have I made, you know, do I have a controlling idea? Do I know what I'm writing about? Do I know why it's about? Have I mapped that value to the value to the audience? Do I know the emotions? I want my audience to experience as a result of what they're reading. Like, yeah, I mean, no reason that couldn't be a checklist. There's nothing wrong with that. Last question. When do you stop editing and not let it get out of control trying to be perfect? Well, the art of email was scheduled to be delivered in March and we shipped it in October. So I don't know, Andre, do you think we have any problem with being perfectionists? You ever get that sense when we're writing? No. God, we're awful about it. Don't, well, here's the caveat. We enjoy that. We, there's a precision that works for us that we reckon we were recovering perfectionist. Lowercase are for recovering. Don't, don't try, don't please don't try to write perfectly. It's, it's, it's a road to nowhere. Get, you know, perfect as the enemy of done, perfect as the enemy of good. You've heard all those phrase, just get something that's 80% quality out the door. But I have had more conversations with clients who have said to me, well, I, you know, I follow Ben Settle and I, you know, I do daily emails to my list. And I just look at them and think to myself, in what world could you ever have imagined? That your audience, relative to what you do is something that you should communicate with them every day. Like it makes no sense whatsoever. And I think in general, I think it is very unusual over the course of measured over the course of months and years that our audience wants to hear from us every day. Yes, it can work in certain circumstances in certain markets. But that to me, that's that's very rare air. I think it's much more effective to have some level of humility and say, where do I fit in my prospects world? And what is the frequency with which I like what makes the most sense for them? And maybe that's just the wrong way to look at it. You know, it might be a better way to look at it. But that's just my reaction to it. So I don't know what time that didn't say that. All right, let's go on to the next question from Guillermo. This is a nine point puzzle section. So I'm pulled in two directions when I read this. Everyone tells us to understand our avatar research, etc. Yet if you think of the nine point puzzle, you're saying to not be trapped by your assumptions. How do these two concepts fit together when building all this out? I think you've made a leap here, right? The maybe maybe I'll talk myself into making the same leap with you. But the nine point puzzle is really just this recognition that if if when you broaden the scope of what you're looking at, when you expand the edges of what you're looking at, more solutions become available to you. Right. And that our assumptions can trap us. You know, unspoken assumptions can trap us to seeing fewer opportunities. So this idea of to understand your avatar and your research, there's no reason to be trapped by that. Right. I mean, yes, you should understand what resonates with your audience. And we're not suggesting that that's going to somehow lock you in. It just I'm not seeing the relationship between those two things. So I think the way I would approach having you think about this is to say, look at your avatar, research your audience, but allow yourself to put that in a broad context. For example, recognizing that your audience can only tell you what they know that they want you to tell them. They can't tell you what they don't know that you know that you can tell them. So the nine point puzzle would suggest that when you're thinking about creating a world for your audience, that you don't limit that creation only to what they can tell you that they want. That you also need to include the things that you know that they don't know because they don't know they don't have the ability to tell you about something they don't know that they want. Right. That's really an application of the nine point puzzle, I think here. Anything to add to that, Andre, if I have no idea if I made any sense. Yeah, and it does. And I think the point of the nine point puzzle wasn't about listening to what the market wants and giving him this thing. I mean, I don't know of anybody that comes into someone's world and says, oh, you know, I wish that was a sales funnel. I would have felt better about it, but it's not a sales funnel. And then they email you. Just laughed out loud and said that I muted myself. Yeah, I mean, this is this is more meta than that. This is the stuff that you do. Ex, you know, external to surveying a market, understanding what they want and then expressing that in a way that's beneficial to them and to you. It's two different things. I mean, think about it this way, too. Right. Like it, I mean, from the perspective of the nine point puzzle, if we if we decided to create an email course on the assumption that if you don't ask people to buy, they won't. And like everything that followed, like the course would be radically different. The recommendations would be radically different if we just had that assumption. The moment we challenge that assumption, like the possibility space expanded as a result, all kinds of new opportunities become available to us. Now, not we don't just embrace them because like, oh, look, this seems possible. And yes, we need to pair that with, okay, what does this mean? We have to actually look at it. But we your nine point puzzle just tells us to expand our possibility space when we look for things. That's really, I think, all that it's saying. I am super psyched. Super psyched. I think the simple answer is we don't really distinguish. We show up and we share and emails that we get that end up in help scouts. We don't go and check if someone is a prospect or a customer and then respond. We just respond. In that moment, if it's not a long email, if it's a long email, it normally gets pushed aside just for a bit longer because there's an opportunity cost and a time constraint that we will get to every single email. But yeah, we don't go check and if it's a customer, we answer differently. Context matters. If the question is about becoming a customer, then that's important to know. If it's already a customer and they're asking a question that's inside of a course, that's different. But I think for the most part, people just engage with us and we engage back. They've taken this commitment and raised their hand and opted in and they want to know more. At that point, they're inside of our inner world. Then there's obviously different layers to that because at some point, they're going to feel pulled to invest in one of our courses and then that's even deeper down the rabbit hole. That's just how we've chosen to show up and we don't really distinguish what we're going to say other than being the most helpful. I think that's my answer. Yeah, I think everything you said is what I would say. I don't have much to add other than to say. I think there's a couple of things to consider here too. We are deliberately expanding, I don't want to say building because Andre built this business and then I came in later and I'm helping, we're building something new together. So I don't know what the proper term for that evolution would be, but we have a vision, we believe in craft. So we want the ability to be able to interact with our audience, customers, whatever paying, not paying. So the business, again, this is a second order thinking thing. If we had a million customers, we couldn't do that. And just to be clear, if you get an email that has our name on it, we wrote it. I know lots of people, and this is not meant to be disparaging, but I know lots of people out there in our little corner of the universe when they have people who write as them and you get an email. And I had that experience once of interacting with someone who I thought I was interacting with based on the signature on the email, only to find out later when that person and I became friends that he had never seen those emails. We had never communicated and that was really, really gross, really gross. And he's a great person. It was a business decision. So for us, we have made this decision. Again, for us, we think about this as being an integrity, but when you get an email from one of us, one of us wrote it. When you get one of my Ramling 45 minute loom videos, I record it. That's harder to fake. That's me. But I think the larger, there are a couple of layers within this. One, this is actually easier. It's a lot easier to not distinguish paying customer versus, I don't want to do that. I don't want to, like someone sends an email and then I've got to go look and get their customer record and screw that. That's no fun. I don't want to. I never want to have a business where I have to do that. So that's a decision we've made about how we want to experience the world. So lots of nuances here. It's easier. It's more authentic to who we are. It does have limitations. We occasionally feel bad because we can't be as responsive as we want to be, like over the 10 days of the art of email enrollment, it was hard to keep up sometimes. And there were times when I felt like I wish I had responded sooner than I eventually did. And it was, I didn't want to give the wrong impression to the person. So it does have consequences, but on balance, we've just made this decision that we want to have a business where we can interact directly with our audience. From my perspective, always beneficial. I always learned something in every interaction with every person. I'm endlessly fascinated by people. And I recognize that someone's giving to me their most precious resource, which is their attention that I want to reflect back at the respect I have for having received that attention. So it just feels like this beautiful dance of life that's going on. When that happens, it doesn't feel like a burden in any way. 99% of the time, sometimes we get dumb questions, frankly. So who knows? Yeah. And I just, someone put Gary Halberst's name in others in the chat. I, I read the question is the best living cooperators. So I should just have mentioned that. I wasn't thinking about Halberst and others. All right. Next question is from Julie. All right. So Julie asks, how do you transition writing to your list from the old style? As if not that style, we advocate in the art of email until you're starting to write with intent of world building. So this is a good question. All questions are good questions. I say that I just, my reaction to it is it's an exciting question to answer. There are two ways to think about this. One is to pull the band aid off, right? And you just start writing a new way. The other way is to kind of transition. I think the larger question to ask yourself is where does, where does your audience come from already? What are their expectations? And then how does that influence the rate, the decision that you're making to switch styles? So and what is the size of your audience? How much of that audience is, are they paying customer? Like all of these questions because if you have a small audience, small list, you're just getting started, you've been writing one way, then I would say just start writing a different way. Like everyone, like send, I would, if you have an existing list, I would kind of tell people, you know, there's this, I'm gonna, I'd like to sort of sell the idea of a relationship building series and then have everybody on your list go through that series. And then on the other side of it is your art of email inspired approach. That's one way to do it. And then everybody has kind of a, a standardized starting point. And you've sort of informed them what to expect and recognize in religious postage, just the 600 people that some percentage of those people are not going to pay attention, either right now or ever, you're going to lose some of those 600 because you had a different voice before that's fine. Like this, it's okay. Conversely, you might have a lot of people sending you feedback and this is amazing. And thanks so much. I mean, who knows, like you're going to get a reaction. But then that's, that's sort of, that's one way to think about it. A second way to think about it is more subtle and nuanced, which is look at how you've been communicating and then choose something, maybe a soap opera series, you know, maybe you create a newsletter, but that's something your voice is a little bit different. And that voice, I don't want to see new versus old, but it's there's a, you're making a gradual move. I guess a third way, maybe, there are probably 30 ways. The third way I would think about this too is, perhaps you take a soap opera series, let's just for the sake of argument, say it's 10 emails. And over the course of those 10 emails, you're transitioning from current voice to new voice. And because that happens over that 10 email period of time, it's so subtle that it never feels jarring to the audience. They just have the sense at the end, like they're, they're more engaged with you at the end, but they don't really know how it happened. It just happened. I think that's what I would do, not necessarily 10, but I would, I would imagine this, I would, I would craft a soap opera series where the underlying intention in my mind is, how do I take everyone who was on my list now and turn them into perfect prospects or customer and how do we want to think about it? What would, what would I need to do to craft that experience and then make that the soap opera series, create that and send everybody through it. And so that you know on the other side of it, like that transformation, like that's, that's how I think I would approach it. Andre, your thoughts on that? I'd probably, I'd probably write a soap opera series, you know, five, 10 emails. And it'll take somebody on a journey. It won't sell anything. It will just, it will be something of value packaged up in, in a serialized narrative, in your new narrative, serialized voice. And then I would send out a bridge email and, and ask people to raise their hand if they're interested in hearing this thing that you've created. You could say I'm trying something new would love for you to send me feedback if you like it or if you don't like it, let me know as well. And click this link and it'll hit your inbox and it's, it'll last five emails, 10 emails, whatever it is. And then that's it. People will go through it. Not all, you know, 600 people won't go through it, but percentage will. And you'll, and you'll get feedback and it'll either be overwhelmingly positive or negative or something. And if it's positive, it'll, it'll, it'll light you up. And, you know, maybe then the next thing would be to do your relationship building series in the same tone. And that's how you can gradually build, build your thing out. But, you know, sometimes it's nice to get that, that squirt of dopamine. And you can do that nicely with a, with a soap opera series. There it is. I've started a trend here. Squirt. Never trust the dopamine. We're down to our last question. Excellent. All right. Here's the question. Mary, I think I would pronounce that correctly. With the art of email definition of what a customer is, how do you communicate with people who are not a good fit for you, but yet consume your content either on social media or end up in your email list or reply to emails? And the context is that she has a decent social media presence and it started to happen a lot. And sometimes it feels bad for not engaging the same way with those people. And so everyone else she can put considers to be a good fit. Should I treat every follower and subscriber as a customer, even when they're not a good fit? We deal with this a lot too, incidentally. And we get lots of people who send us emails and through any variety of indicators. Usually it's some variation of I want to learn how to make money really fast, which course is the best for it. That's usually a good indicator. But we have a fair, I don't know, 5% to 10% of our inquiries are what would fit this definition. They're not our people. They're not our prospects. We're not going to make them happy if we sell them something. And what we do is we just point them to our free resources. And they still get a personal response in general. But we'll say that often it's surprising. We actively discourage people from buying a lot. Because buying for them very clearly from what they've shared with us is not the best solution that we can offer them. So what we do instead often is we will say, essentially, I appreciate you reaching out, recognizing that they are sharing their attention. That's important. And say, if it's something like how do I make the fastest, we're very upfront. So we're not in the make money quick business. We don't have anything that fits that really well. However, we generally don't want to leave people empty handed. However, we have, if you are interested in internet marketing, we have a tremendous number of resources, including free courses. And then we'll share a link either to the free resources section of our site and maybe draw attention to a starting place like our 2020 year review is a good place to get started. So we do engage. We don't just look at something and not a good fit, forget it. And it doesn't scale. We can't do this with a million prospects. But the way our business is constructed, we have something else. And I guess the last point I'll make on this is you don't have to, just because we do that doesn't mean you need to do that. You might make a decision that to protect your attention and for you to be able to create the most value in the world, you need to not engage with that. Particularly, social media, I don't engage with any social media. I don't look at comments on ads or anything else, because despite my best efforts of not being affected by it, it irritates the hell out of me. So I just don't engage with it. So be cautious of what you do let into your life, because it can throw you for a loop. So you don't owe anybody that it's a choice that you can make and make the choice that's best for you, I guess is the message. Well, Andrew, anything to how would you approach that one? I heard Seth Godin express a version of this a long, long time ago, maybe a decade or plus ago, and I loved his answer and I've never forgotten it. And I think one of the attendees at a conference that he was at asked him the question is like, why aren't you on social media or why aren't you on Twitter or wherever he was? And he says, his answer, which is a lot more eloquent than I'm about to express is basically he has a certain level of, there's an opportunity cost of being on a place and he has a certain level of quality or he shows up and he puts a certain he has a certain amount of time and that time allows him to only do so many things and he can't do everything. He can only do some things and of all those things, he would rather show up on his website and write his daily email and then obviously his books and things like that. So he's not on Twitter because he doesn't have the time to be on Twitter and he's not just going to be on Twitter to post little tweets pointing to his articles because that's not showing up on social media in the way that where you can establish relationships. So he's, he refuses just to jump on social media just to post links to stuff that he's writing. If he's going to use social media or use Twitter, he wants to engage with people and he just, you know, he said he doesn't have the bandwidth for that. So he doesn't do it. So if you want to care about me, as Seth Gordon, you come to my website, you opt in and you get my emails and or you buy my books and that's how you care about me. And so yeah, I've never forgotten that and I think there's a, I'm just going to jump on to my hajusya very briefly and you know, there's many people that think just because there is social media that they should use social media and just because social media is Pinterest and Twitter and Facebook and Instagram and and and TikTok, they need to be everywhere. And the only way you can really be everywhere is by putting thin pieces of stuff out there. And yeah, so I don't, I don't agree with that. I mean, I think if you get, if you want to use social media as a channel in pick one or two, you know, based on how much time you can, you can give pick one or two, where you feel that you can have relationship, you know, where you can engage with an audience in a meaningful way that obviously then points to, to your main world. And if that's only one social media channel, well, then that's the one social media channel that you should be on and ignore all the rest as if they don't even exist. That's our debt and I think other people do that and there's people like Dave Perel, who, you know, he's on Twitter, he gets so much traffic from Twitter because he's there engaging all the time. And then he writes long form content, which is on his website. And that's, that's, that's a strategy, but he's not on TikTok or wherever else, you know, he's, he's, he's chosen the places that he's willing to show up and spend the time to engage with an audience. And, and he gets the reward from that. Yeah, I think that's all come off. It's a type my AirPods, my AirPods are dead. So I think I'm done speaking here. So I'm literally like 5%.
